title: These sectors are still returning cash to shareholders amid AI buildout needs – BofA
content:
[Three stacks of coloured coins]
Richard Drury

As artificial intelligence capital expenditures continue to surge, a stark divide has emerged between technology giants strapped for cash and old economy sectors flush with returns for investors.

According to a BofA Global Research note, with more cash required for the AI buildout, cyclical value sectors like energy (XLE [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/XLE]), (AMLP [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/AMLP]), (VDE [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/VDE]), (XOP [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/XOP]) and financials (XLF [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/XLF]), (VFH [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/VFH]), (IYF [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/IYF]), (FNCL [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/FNCL]) are outperforming their tech counterparts in shareholder returns.

[Energy and Financials offer the highest total shareholder yield (div yield + net buyback yield)]
Energy and Financials offer the highest total shareholder yield (div yield + net buyback yield) (FactSet, BofA US Equity & Quant Strategy) 

Big tech companies find themselves locked in an expensive AI arms race that has severely constrained their ability to reward shareholders. Savita Subramanian, head of U.S. Equity & Quantitative Strategy for BofA Global Research, observed that "buybacks are hampered by waning cash flow, which has dramatically decoupled from strong earnings, which have been inflated by investment income."

These hyperscalers cannot cut capital expenditures without risking their competitive position in the AI race, leaving shareholders waiting for returns.

In contrast, value sectors that were starved of capital following the global financial crisis have emerged as disciplined, lean operators.

According to Subramanian, "cash return is best in old economy cyclicals like financials, energy, materials (XLB [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/XLB]), (VAW [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/VAW]), (IYM [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/IYM]), (FXZ [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/FXZ]) and worst in hyperscalers and discretionary (XLY [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/XLY]), (VCR [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/VCR]), (FXD [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/FXD]), (FDIS [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/FDIS])."

These sectors have maintained low leverage and are now positioned to reward investors while tech companies pour resources into AI infrastructure.

Artificial Intelligence/Robotics ETFs: (AIQ [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/AIQ]), (BOTZ [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/BOTZ]), (DTEC [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/DTEC]), (WTAI [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/WTAI]), (XAIX [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/XAIX]), (WISE [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/WISE]), (GINN [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/GINN]), (ROBT [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/ROBT]), (TECB [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/TECB]), (XT [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/XT]), (THNQ [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/THNQ]), and (CHAT [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/CHAT]).

AI infrastructure ETFs: (AIPO [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/AIPO]), (TCAI [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/TCAI]), (IAINF [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/IAINF])

title: AI power demand: Utilities cashing in on data center expansion
content:
[Electrical Power Lines]
LPETTET

INVESTORS SHOULD KNOW: AI data center expansion is creating a surge in power demand. Utilities and independent power producers are building long-term revenue visibility through large-scale electricity supply agreements with technology customers.

BACKGROUND

The rapid buildout of AI infrastructure requires enormous amounts of electricity. Data centers running large language models and GPU clusters consume power at a scale that strains existing grid capacity, pushing technology companies to secure dedicated power supply deals directly with utilities and power producers.

This dynamic has turned power supply into one of the most discussed bottlenecks in the AI buildout. Utilities that can sign long-term load agreements with data center operators gain multi-year revenue visibility. Independent power producers with flexible generation assets, including natural gas, nuclear, and renewables, are also positioning to serve this demand.

STOCKS TO WATCH

Key publicly traded companies in this space include Bloom Energy Corporation (BE [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/BE]), Talen Energy Corporation (TLN [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/TLN]), Oklo Inc. (OKLO [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/OKLO]), and The AES Corporation (AES [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/AES]) — each classified as AI power and energy suppliers. American Electric Power Company, Inc. (AEP [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/AEP]) is a major regulated utility with direct exposure to large-load data center agreements. TransAlta Corporation (TAC [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/TAC]) and Power Solutions International, Inc. (PSIX [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/PSIX]) also operate in the AI power supply ecosystem. Fermi Inc. (FRMI [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/FRMI]) and Capstone Energy+, Inc. (CGEH [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/CGEH]) round out the publicly traded names in this theme.

On the ETF side, the Defiance AI & Power Infrastructure ETF (AIPO [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/AIPO]) and the Global X Data Center & Digital Infrastructure ETF (DTCR [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/DTCR]) offer thematic exposure. The Virtus Reaves Utilities ETF (UTES [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/UTES]) appears in both the AI power theme and the broader utility ETF universe.

KEY TAKEAWAYS

 	* AI data center growth is driving a measurable increase in electricity demand, creating new revenue opportunities for utilities and power producers willing to sign large-load supply agreements.
 	* Executed multi-year contracts with data center operators are becoming a key indicator of forward revenue visibility for power companies in this space.
 	* Both individual stocks and ETFs focused on utilities and electrification infrastructure offer exposure to this theme, spanning regulated utilities, independent power producers, and thematic funds.

NEWS ROUNDUP

Recent developments show utilities actively converting AI-driven power demand into concrete, long-term supply commitments. The examples below illustrate how contracted load agreements are translating into measurable capacity additions and revenue ramps for power companies.

 	* ALLIANT ENERGY — The company has disclosed five fully executed data center agreements totaling approximately 3.4 GW [https://seekingalpha.com/news/4584289-alliant-outlines-7-percent-earnings-cagr-for-2027minus-2029-as-contracted-data-center-demand], including a new 370 MW electric service agreement with a ramp targeted by the end of 2030. Management cited confidentiality constraints on certain economics, but the agreements represent staged capacity additions and multi-year planning commitments tied directly to data center load growth.
 	* POWERBANK CORPORATION — The company signed a modular data center joint development agreement with Nodiac.ai [https://seekingalpha.com/pr/20566340-modular-data-center-joint-development-agreement-signed-between-powerbank-corporation-and], pairing PowerBank's solar and battery storage systems with Nodiac's co-location model. The approach targets behind-the-meter power delivery at existing generation sites, bypassing lengthy grid interconnection timelines to bring power directly to digital infrastructure.

AI POWER SUPPLIERS IN FOCUS

The screener below identifies stocks classified as AI power and energy suppliers, ranked by relevance to the AI bottlenecks: power and energy theme, with the most theme-aligned companies appearing first.

The next screener provides broader sector context. It filters companies across electric, gas, and water utility industries and ranks them from highest to lowest by Quant Rating.

{"type":"screeners","props":{"type":"stocks","name":"Top Utility Power Stocks","filter":{"industry_id":{"in":[55101010,55102010,55103010,55104010,55105010,55105020]}},"sort":"-quant_rating"},"data":[{},{},{},{},{},{},{},{},{"slug":"so","quant_rating":null,"industry":"Electric Utilities","company_name":"The Southern Company","has_virtual_analysis":true},{"slug":"esocf","quant_rating":null,"industry":"Electric Utilities","company_name":"Enel SpA","has_virtual_analysis":true}],"show_in_screener_page":true}

The AI power supplier stock screener includes ten companies spanning fuel cells, nuclear, natural gas, and regulated electric utilities — reflecting the range of generation technologies being positioned to serve data center load growth.

Many of the stocks already highlighted earlier appear in the screener. Meanwhile, AEP appears in both the thematic and sector screeners, bridging the regulated utility world and the AI power supply theme.

The utility sector screener covers electric, gas, and water utilities across multiple geographies. The screener surfaces some large U.S. players, along with a number of major international names: Iberdrola, S.A. (IBDRY [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/IBDRY])(IBDSF [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/IBDSF]), COPEL (ELPC [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/ELPC]), Enel Chile S.A. (ENIC [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/ENIC]), AXIA Energia SA (AXIA [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/AXIA]), American Electric Power (AEP [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/AEP]), New Jersey Resources Corp. (NJR [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/NJR]), The Southern Co. (SO [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SO]), and Enel SpA (ESOCF [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/ESOCF]).

AI POWER ETFS AND UTILITY FUNDS

The screener below identifies traditional ETFs classified under the AI power and energy supplier theme, ranked by relevance to the theme.

The AI power ETF screener returns three funds: the Virtus Reaves Utilities ETF (UTES [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/UTES]), the Defiance AI & Power Infrastructure ETF (AIPO [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/AIPO]), and the Global X Data Center & Digital Infrastructure ETF (DTCR [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/DTCR]). Each is classified as an AI power and energy supplier, offering thematic exposure through a single fund structure.

The broader utility ETF screener covers ten funds across the utilities asset class. The results include electrification-focused funds alongside broad utility index products. Among the names represented: Tema Electrification ETF (VOLT [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/VOLT]), Virtus Reaves Utilities ETF (UTES [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/UTES]), First Trust Utilities AlphaDEX Fund ETF (FXU [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/FXU]), Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight Utilities ETF (RSPU [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/RSPU]), ALPS Electrification Infrastructure ETF (ELFY [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/ELFY]), State Street Utilities Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLU [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/XLU]), Global X US Electrification ETF (ZAP [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/ZAP]), Vanguard Utilities Index Fund ETF (VPU [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/VPU]), iShares US Utilities ETF (IDU [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/IDU]), and Fidelity MSCI Utilities Index ETF (FUTY [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/FUTY]).

title: Defiance 隆重推出歐洲首個記憶體 UCITS ETF (DRAM)
content:
Defiance 推出 Defiance Memory UCITS ETF（股票代號：DRAM），擴充其在歐洲的 ETF 陣容。此 ETF 旨在讓投資者參與投資於記憶體半導體及數據儲存系統開發、製造、商業化及儲存等範疇的企業。在美國，專注於記憶體的 ETF 已累積約 200 億美元的管理資產 (AUM)1。此 ETF 目前於 Xetra 及 Borsa Italiana 上市，亦將加入倫敦證券交易所。

邁阿密, June  20, 2026  (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- Defiance ETFs 隆重宣佈推出歐洲首個記憶體 ETF——Defiance Memory UCITS ETF（股票代號：DRAM）。 此基金旨在讓投資者參與投資於記憶體半導體及數據儲存系統開發、製造、商業化及儲存等範疇的企業。

Defiance Memory UCITS ETF
ISIN: IE000CEUZ052
TER: 0.69%

交易所Bloomberg 股票代號SEDOL交易貨幣XetraDRAM GYBVVG296歐元Borsa ItalianaDRAM IMBVVG2B8美元

記憶體價格正在攀升。 AI、雲端運算和數據中心的需求，正佔據越來越多先進記憶體產能；主要製造商亦重視高頻寬記憶體和伺服器級 DRAM（動態隨機存取記憶體）等較高利潤的領域，多於較為商品化的消費應用層面2。

此轉變正為整個科技供應鏈帶來壓力。 當供應流向 AI 基建及超大規模數據中心之際，日常裝置的製造商亦面臨成本上漲及供應緊縮的雙重壓力。

預計今年記憶體將無法滿足全球需求3。Gartner 指出，DRAM 與固態硬碟 (SSD) 的價格，至 2026 年底或可飆升達 130%4。

至今只有在美國才可透過 ETF 投資記憶體板塊，當地相關 ETF 的資產規模目前約為 200 億美元5。Defiance Memory UCITS ETF 旨在讓歐洲投資者把握記憶體領域的機遇，而該領域未來需要擴張才能追上 AI 帶動的需求。

這是 Defiance 自今年初進軍歐洲 UCITS ETF 市場以來第四款推出的產品。

Defiance UCITS 產品陣容股票代號Defiance AI & Power Infrastructure UCITS ETFAIPODefiance Memory UCITS ETFDRAMDrone UCITS ETFDRONUkraine Reconstruction UCITS ETFUKRN

Defiance ETFs 投資總監 Sylvia Jablonski 表示：「記憶體是 AI 經濟的根基。 無論是模型訓練、推論工作負載，抑或超大規模數據中心的擴展，皆要仰賴 DRAM、HBM 及先進儲存技術。 DRAM 讓歐洲投資者能以規則為本的方式，直接投資於 AI 價值鏈這個環節，另亦補充了現有 AIPO 所提供的電力基建投資渠道。」

HANetf 共同創辦人兼聯席行政總裁 Hector McNeil 表示：「我們很高興能與 Defiance 合作，推出 Defiance Memory UCITS ETF。 此 ETF 所覆蓋的行業，近期隨著 AI 及其基建崛起而呈現顯著增長。 此 ETF 尤其能與 Defiance 的 AIPO ETF 互補，其中 AIPO 提供 AI 建設背後電力基建的投資渠道。」

有關完整的基金詳情，包括招股章程及關鍵資訊文件，請瀏覽 hanetf.com。

關於 Defiance ETFs

Defiance 於 2018 年創立，為頂尖的 ETF 發行機構，專門從事主題式、收益型及槓桿型 ETF。 公司管理逾 75 隻 ETF，目標是精準投資高增長範疇，涵蓋 AI 基建、量子運算、無人機及現代戰爭，以至其他新興技術。

關於 HANetf

HANetf 是 UCITS ETF 的獨立供應商，與資產管理公司攜手，為歐洲 ETF 投資者提供獨特、現代而創新的投資選擇。 透過我們的白牌 ETF 平台，HANetf 為資產管理公司提供涵蓋營運、監管、分銷及市場推廣的完整方案，讓其能夠推出和管理 UCITS ETF。 www.hanetf.com

媒體聯絡人

Brenda Hentschel | bhentschel@gregoryagency.com | 201.705.3758

歐洲媒體查詢：
意大利：Elena Soffientini，Mymediarelation | soffientini@mymediarelation.it | +39 375 670 62 07
德國：Caroline Chojnowski，Public Imaging | Caroline.Chojnowski@publicimaging.de | +49 (0)40-401 999 - 23

重要資訊

在英國發佈的通訊

本資訊專為專業投資者而編寫，惟本資訊中所列的 ETC 及 ETF（「產品」）在部分司法管轄區或可供任何投資者認購。 請向您的經紀商或中介機構確認，相關產品在您所屬的司法管轄區是否可供投資，以及是否符合您的投資風險屬性。

本文件可能載有前瞻性陳述，包括我們對某些資產類別表現的信念或當前預期。 前瞻性陳述涉及若干風險、不確定因素及假設。 無法保證該等陳述準確無誤，實際結果可能與該等陳述的預測出現重大出入。 因此，讀者應注意切勿過份倚賴這些前瞻性陳述。

本文件內容僅供參考及閣下內部使用，並不構成投資建議、推薦、投資研究、出售要約或招攬購買任何產品或進行任何投資的要約。

投資交易所買賣產品的回報，取決於相關資產類別扣除費用後的表現，惟預期不會完全緊貼該表現。 該等產品涉及多項風險，包括但不限於指數（就 ETF 而言）或相關資產類別及貨幣的不利價格變動所致的一般市場風險，以及流動性、營運、法律和監管風險。 此外，就加密貨幣 ETC 而言，此類數碼資產極其波動，表現無法預測。

本文件所載資料並非（且在任何情況下均不應被視為）在美國或其任何州份或地區進行證券公開發售的廣告或任何其他推廣步驟，原因是發行方（定義見下文）或其產品均未獲授權或註冊在當地分銷，而任何發行方的招股書亦未有向任何證券委員會或監管機構提交。 本文件的任何文件或資訊均不得（直接或間接）被帶入、傳輸或分發至美國。 各發行方或其發行的任何證券，均未曾且將來亦不會根據《1933 年美國證券法》(United States Securities Act of 1933) 或《1940 年投資公司法》(Investment Company Act of 1940) 註冊，亦未根據任何適用的州證券法規取得資格。

發行方：
1. HANetf ICAV 及 HANetf ICAV II 是愛爾蘭開放式集體資產管理工具，並根據各 ETF 的相關招股章程及補充文件（已獲愛爾蘭中央銀行（「CBI」）批准）所載條款，擔任 ETF 的發行方（各自稱為「ETF 招股章程」，統稱 「ETF 招股章程」）。 投資者應於投資前閱覽相關 ETF 招股章程的最新版本，並查閱相關 ETF 招股章程中「風險因素」一節，以了解投資 ETF 所涉風險的更多詳情。 任何投資決定均應以 ETF 招股章程所載的資料為依據。

2. HANetf ETC Securities plc 是在愛爾蘭註冊成立的公共有限公司，根據經愛爾蘭中央銀行批准的基礎招股章程及相關系列的最終條款（「ETC 證券文件」）發行產品，是貴金屬 ETC 的發行方。 投資者應於投資前閱覽 ETC 證券文件的最新版本，並查閱基礎招股章程中「風險因素」一節，以了解投資 ETC 所涉風險的更多詳情。 任何投資決定均應以 ETF 證券文件所載的資料為依據。

3. Bitwise Europe GmbH 是根據德意志聯邦共和國法律註冊成立的有限公司，依據經聯邦金融監管局（「BaFin」）批准的招股章程及最終條款（「加密貨幣招股章程」）發行，是 ETCM ETC 的發行方。 投資者應於投資前閱覽加密貨幣招股章程的最新版本，並查閱加密貨幣招股章程中「風險因素」一節，以了解投資當中所載 ETC 所涉風險的更多詳情。 任何投資決定均應以加密貨幣招股章程所載的資料為依據。

4. HANetf Multi-Asset ETC Issuer plc 是在澤西島註冊成立的公共公司，依據經瑞典金融監管局（Sw. Finansinspektionen）（「SFSA」）及英國金融行為監管局（「FCA」）批准的基礎招股章程及相關系列的最終條款（「多元資產 ETC 證券文件」）發行，是與多種相關資產掛鈎並以其作擔保的 ETC 之發行方。 投資者應於投資前閱覽 ETC 證券文件的最新版本，並查閱相關基礎招股章程中「風險因素」一節，以了解投資 ETC 所涉風險的更多詳情。 任何投資決定均應以 ETF 證券文件所載的資料為依據。

相關的 ETF 招股章程、ETC 證券文件、多元資產 ETC 證券文件及加密貨幣招股章程，均可於 www.hanetf.com 下載。

投資任何產品的決策及金額，應先諮詢獨立的投資、稅務及法律意見，並顧及您的個人具體狀況。 我們無法控制第三方網站的內容，亦不對此負責。

我們相信本文件資料基於可靠來源，惟不保證其準確無誤。 文中所述觀點乃 HANetf 於發佈當下的意見，日後或會更改。 Privium 及 HANetf 概不負責因本通訊資料的準確性、完整性或使用所致的任何損失，包括任何相應損失。

title: Burgers, not bonds: what Americans really search for in business news
content:
[Hand Man in car receiving coffee in drive thru fast food restaurant. Staff serving takeaway order for driver in delivery window. Drive through and takeaway for buy fast food for protect covid19.]
kckate16/iStock via Getty Images

As Wall Street speculates on whether Kevin Warsh can cut the fed funds rate this year, Main Street says that can wait — the Taco Bell drive-thru is closing.

If you consume a lot of financial media, you might believe the average American spends their days tracking fractional basis point shifts or parsing abstract macroeconomic growth indicators. But pull back the digital curtain on six months of raw, aggregated search engine data leading into midyear 2026, and a far more pragmatic reality emerges. 

Most people do not treat business news as an academic exercise; they consume it as a tool for personal defense.

The data reveals an economy undergoing a massive, synchronized transition toward structural self-preservation. Reactionary anxieties over winter inflation and fast-food price shocks have evolved into highly calculated consumer strategies. Today, readers are actively tracking the physical limitations of the tech sector, specifically watching how massive AI datacenters are integrating into localized energy grids and micro-reactors.

They are preparing for strict financial calendar milestones like the major July 1 student loan restructuring. And rather than simply lamenting inflation, they are actively outmaneuvering it—evidenced by a massive, programmatic surge in tracking bulk-buy discount drops and wholesale club price cuts.

Ultimately, Americans are watching the economy not to monitor Wall Street’s wealth, but to fortify their own.

The data below was compiled from an extensive AI analysis of more than 18,000 distinct daily and hourly trending search volume datasets in the United States, tracking user intent from late December 2025 through June 2026. Queries were normalized, filtered for explicit business and economic intent, and categorized across five distinct structural macro-narratives to map out true consumer attention share as we approach the midyear mark.

 		RANK
 		CORE NARRATIVE CATEGORY
 		MIDYEAR SEARCH SHARE (%)
 		KEY MULTI-MONTH QUERY ANCHORS
 		EVOLVING CONSUMER/READER PSYCHE

 		1
 		AI INFRASTRUCTURE & COMPUTING POWER
 		29.0%
 		Nvidia (NVDA), Palantir (PLTR), OpenAI Stargate, Google Project Genie, Vistra (VST), Antares Mark-0 Reactor, Claude CoWork
 		Transitioned from general software curiosity to tracking physical power-grid infrastructure, utility constraints, and real automation milestones.

 		2
 		EVERYDAY WALLET DEFENSE & PAYOUTS
 		23.0%
 		Where's My Refund, 2026 Tax Brackets, July 1 Student Loan Changes, Trump Credit Card Interest Cap, State Farm $5B Payout, Wells Fargo Settlement
 		Intensive focus on personal household liquidity via seasonal tax returns, legal settlements, and navigating strict regulatory calendar cliffs.

 		3
 		WARTIME COMMODITIES & GLOBAL CHOKE POINTS
 		18.0%
 		Strait of Hormuz Closed / Oil Shipping, Finished Steel/Aluminum 25% Tariffs, Persian Gulf Smelter Strike, Exxon (XOM), Lockheed (LMT)
 		Aggressive tracking of international raw industrial material supply lines, trade barriers, and global shipping lanes directly impacted by military action.

 		4
 		CORPORATE & MAIN STREET STRUCTURAL SHIFTS
 		16.0%
 		Fast Food Closures (Pizza Hut/Arby's) (YUM [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/YUM]), Costco Kirkland Price Drops (COST [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/COST]), Macy's Closing (M [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/M]), Circle Furniture Shuttering, Pinterest (PINS [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/PINS]) Layoffs
 		Evolved from reactive economic panic over brand failures to active cost avoidance and inflation-hedging via warehouse clubs and grocery price drops.

 		5
 		ALTERNATIVE CURRENCIES & SAFE HAVENS
 		14.0%
 		Gold Price $5,100/oz. (GLD [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/GLD]), Silver Trust (SLV) Volatility, Bitcoin 16-Month Low / Crash (BTC-USD [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/BTC-USD]), Ripple (XRP-USD) Speculation, Coinbase (COIN [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/COIN])
 		A persistent, defensive flight to alternative stores of hard value as geopolitical tensions and macro trust challenges push traditional markets lower.

title: Out of mega-cap and into financials and healthcare, says Janus Henderson’s Jeremiah Buckley
content:
[An arrow points up]
Richard Drury

Jeremiah Buckley, portfolio manager at Janus Henderson, sees significant opportunities for investors to diversify beyond mega-cap stocks (MAGS [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/MAGS]), (MAGX [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/MAGX]), pointing to attractive valuations in sectors like financial services (XLF [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/XLF]) and healthcare (XLV [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/XLV]) that have lagged the AI-driven rally.

In an interview with CNBC, he noted that many stocks in sectors such as airlines (JETS [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/JETS]), homebuilders (XHB [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/XHB]), and retail (XRT [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/XRT]), (RTH [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/RTH]) have been “left behind” during the surge in semiconductor (XMH), (SOXX [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SOXX]) (XSD [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/XSD]) and AI infrastructure (AIPO [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/AIPO]), (IAINF [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/IAINF]) names, and are now "trading below historical multiples."

Buckley highlighted the earnings potential outside the largest technology companies, emphasizing that his portfolio offers broad-based growth.

"The median name in our portfolio has 14% earnings growth this year, and so it's much broader than the major mega caps or also the semiconductor names that are driving their earnings growth at the index," he said.

Within the financial sector (XLF [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/XLF]), (VFH [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/VFH]), (IYF [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/IYF]), Buckley expressed particular interest in capital markets companies like Goldman Sachs (GS [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/GS]) and Morgan Stanley (MS [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/MS]).

He suggested the upcoming SpaceX IPO could serve as a catalyst for the market, calling it "an important milestone for the IPO market."

On inflation concerns, Buckley acknowledged that investors need to watch the relationship between CPI and wage growth carefully.

"We'd like to see wage growth, real wage growth, whereas at that CPI level, we're not seeing it," he explained, while noting that opportunities remain in sectors that can deliver strong earnings despite the economic backdrop.

Regarding rising interest rates, Buckley said current levels are not yet impactful enough to drive significant changes in corporate investment.

However, he warned that if the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/US10Y]) approaches 5%, "that might start to disturb people."

He added that stabilization and eventual reversal on the inflation side will be necessary for the market to maintain its footing.

title: Fastest Growing ETFs of 2026
content:
ETF Investing Tools

Here at ETF.com, we talk a lot about flows into and out of ETFs. Each day, each week and each month, we put together lists of the exchange-traded funds that garner the largest inflows and the largest outflows of assets—an indication of where investors are putting their money to work.

Most of the time, the same batch of ETFs finds itself on these lists. These funds are usually super liquid, extraordinarily cheap and have billions of dollars in assets. For example, the $983 billion Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO) and the $144 billion State Street SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF (SPYM) are among the ETFs that have had the largest inflows so far this year.

No matter how you slice it, their respective year-to-date inflows of $78 billion and $36 billion are sizable. That said, VOO and SPYM aren't necessarily the ETFs growing the fastest.

On an absolute basis they are, but not on a percentage basis. Since the start of the year, VOO has grown from $833 billion to $983 billion—a combination of inflows and price appreciation—equal to a percentage increase of 18%. SPYM has gone from $97 billion to $144 billion, an increase of 49%.

Both have seen impressive growth, especially for their size, but they are nowhere close to the top of the heap.

On a percentage basis, there are myriad ETFs that have grown much faster in 2026. These are ETFs that almost never make our flows lists because they are starting from a much smaller asset base.

In this article, we'll take a look at these fast-growing funds, which, in many cases, are flying under the radar of the ETF investing public at large.

Starting Point

Admittedly, it's much easier for a small fund to register a big percentage increase in its assets. A fund with only $1 million in assets under management simply has to grow to $2 million for its assets to double. Is that noteworthy? Not really.

How about a $1 million fund growing to $50 million? Now that could be noteworthy for some; others might not pay attention until a fund grows even larger, into the hundred-million or even the billion-dollar range.

That's why we've put together two lists: one that showcases the fastest-growing ETFs of the year no matter their starting level of assets, and another that includes ETFs that had $50 million or more in assets at the start of 2026.

Growth is measured in terms of assets as of June 8 compared to what they were at the start of the year. Growth can come from inflows and/or price appreciation.

Interpreting the Growth

ETFs are increasingly being used in model portfolios, which are prepackaged baskets of funds that financial advisors deploy across many client accounts at the same time, so some of the names saw enormous increases in assets thanks to suddenly being included in a popular model portfolio.

Other times, one big institutional investor can move the needle on its own. A single large allocation from a pension fund, hedge fund, or another deep-pocketed buyer can be enough to double or triple the assets of a fund.

Then there are the cases where broad-based interest in a fund catapulted it higher, with no single buyer responsible.

Only ETFs that were trading at the start of the year are included. That means a fund like Roundhill Memory ETF (DRAM), which launched in April, isn't included, even though it's one of the fastest growing funds of all time.

To learn about each fund click the links below and read the individual fund pages. Fund pages can also be accessed by typing ETF.com/ticker into your browser.

Story Continues

title: AI-linked ETFs tumbled in Friday's tech selloff after months of outsized gains
content:
[ETF investment money]
zimmytws/iStock via Getty Images

AI-linked ETFs were among the hardest-hit thematic funds during Friday's technology selloff, as investors dumped semiconductor and AI-related stocks following a Broadcom-driven rout and stronger-than-expected U.S. jobs data that fueled concerns interest rates could remain higher for longer.

The weakness was most pronounced in semiconductor funds, which have been among the biggest beneficiaries of the AI boom. The ProShares Ultra Semiconductors ETF (USD [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/USD]) plunged 16.8%, while the SPDR S&P Semiconductor ETF (XSD [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/XSD]), iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SOXX]), First Trust Nasdaq Semiconductor ETF (FTXL [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/FTXL]), Invesco Semiconductors ETF (PSI [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/PSI]), and Invesco PHLX Semiconductor ETF (SOXQ [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SOXQ]) each fell roughly 10% or more.

Despite the sharp pullback, AI-linked ETFs remain some of the market's top-performing funds in 2026.

title: SpaceX IPO: Every ETF That Will Hold SPCX — and When
content:
ETF Investing Tools

The SpaceX IPO is no longer a distant event — it’s happening June 12. At an estimated $1.75 trillion valuation, SpaceX (ticker: SPCX) is set to become the largest company ever to go public, dwarfing Saudi Aramco’s 2019 IPO and entering the market larger than all but a handful of the world’s biggest corporations.

For ETF investors, the headline isn’t the IPO itself — it’s what happens immediately after. Index rules will automatically funnel tens of billions of dollars from passive funds into SpaceX shares, whether investors realize it or not.

The Forced Buyers: S&P 500 and Nasdaq-100 ETFs

SpaceX is expected to enter the S&P 500 at roughly a 0.5% weight. That sounds small, but the S&P 500 is the benchmark for trillions in indexed assets. Every ETF tracking the index will be required to purchase SpaceX shares upon inclusion. The largest affected funds:

VOO (Vanguard S&P 500 ETF, $1,003.51B AUM): Would need to buy roughly $5B in SpaceX IVV (iShares Core S&P 500 ETF, $859.73B AUM): Similar forced purchase SPY (SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust, $787B AUM): The most liquid S&P 500 ETF SPYM (SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF, $149.68B AUM)

The Nasdaq-100 follows on a slightly different timeline. A revised Nasdaq methodology effective May 1, 2026 allows any newly listed company ranked among the top 40 by market capitalization to enter the Nasdaq-100 after just 15 trading days — with the prior free-float requirement eliminated. That puts SpaceX into the index around July 6, triggering additional forced buying from:

QQQ (Invesco QQQ Trust, $495.70B AUM): The world’s most-traded tech ETF QQQM (Invesco NASDAQ 100 ETF, $98.48B AUM): Lower-cost alternative for long-term investors

All told, analysts estimate that S&P 500 and Nasdaq-100 index funds will need to absorb between $22 billion and $27 billion in SpaceX shares on the days of their respective index inclusions. That forced buying is expected to create significant upward price pressure independent of any fundamental valuation call.

Space-Themed ETFs: Already Positioned

Several thematic ETFs have been building exposure to the SpaceX IPO story for months.

NASA (Tema Space Innovators ETF) is the biggest story. The fund launched in late March 2026 and crossed $1 billion in assets in just 37 trading days. By the end of May it held $2.58 billion — one of the fastest asset-gathering runs in ETF history. NASA is explicitly built around the commercial space economy and is widely seen as the premier vehicle for investors who want concentrated SpaceX exposure alongside other space companies.

Story Continues

UFO (Procure Space ETF) is the original space-focused ETF, with a mandate covering what it calls “the space economy” — satellite communications, launch services, and space infrastructure. UFO crossed $1 billion in assets in late May 2026 and will hold SpaceX post-IPO, having been a key beneficiary of the IPO hype trade.

ARKX (ARK Space Exploration & Innovation ETF) from Cathie Wood’s ARK Invest has supported SpaceX in the private market. ARKX is expected to be among the first active funds to establish a position after the listing.

Pre-IPO ETFs: Already Holding SpaceX Now

Two ETFs offer SpaceX exposure before June 12 through private market holdings:

XOVR (ERShares Private-Public Crossover ETF) has approximately 23% of its portfolio allocated to SpaceX — the largest single holding. XOVR is designed to hold companies through the private-to-public transition, offering daily liquidity and direct exposure that index ETFs can’t provide until after the listing.

AIPO (Defiance AI & Power Infrastructure ETF) holds a basket of assets focused on AI and power infrastructure. It offers broader thematic exposure across multiple holdings rather than concentrated SpaceX exposure.

What This Means for Your Portfolio

If you own VOO, IVV, SPY, QQQ, or virtually any broad U.S. equity ETF, you will own SpaceX automatically after index inclusion — no action required. The question is how much exposure you want beyond the passive baseline.

Investors who want more than the index weight provides have three options: a space-themed ETF like NASA or UFO, a pre-IPO crossover fund like XOVR for exposure now, or simply waiting and buying SPCX shares directly after the IPO opens.

What to watch: The $22–27B in forced index buying is a known event on a known timeline. Markets tend to front-run these inclusions, which means a portion of the “inclusion pop” may already be priced in by June 12. Investors who buy NASA or XOVR purely for the IPO catalyst should factor in that dynamic.

By the Numbers

ETF Type SpaceX Exposure AUM VOO / IVV / SPY S&P 500 index ~0.5% at inclusion $787B–$1,003.51B each QQQ / QQQM Nasdaq-100 index ~0.5–1% at inclusion (~Jul 6) $98.48B–$495.70B NASA Space thematic Direct post-IPO; primary thesis $2.58B UFO Space thematic Direct post-IPO $1.25B ARKX Active space/tech Expected buyer post-IPO $1.09B XOVR Private-public crossover ~23% now (pre-IPO) $2.20B AIPO AI & power infrastructure Thematic holding $784.48M

The SpaceX IPO is the most significant single-company event for the ETF industry in years. Passive investors who own broad index funds will hold SpaceX whether they choose to or not. Those who want more exposure have multiple vehicles to express that view — from the NASA ETF’s concentrated space bet to XOVR’s pre-IPO position to simply waiting for SPCX shares to land in the index.

The June 12 listing is a starting gun, not a finish line. Index inclusion — and the tens of billions in forced buying that comes with it — arrives weeks later.

This article was generated with the assistance of artificial intelligence and reviewed by ETF.com staff.

Nothing on this site should be construed as a recommendation to buy, sell, or hold any security or financial product.
General Investment Risks
Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal. Past performance is not indicative of future results. The value of investments may fluctuate, and investors may receive back less than they originally invested. There is no guarantee that any investment strategy will achieve its objectives.
ETF-Specific Risks
Exchange-traded funds (ETFs) are subject to risks similar to those of stocks and other equity securities. ETF shares are bought and sold at market price, which may differ from the fund's net asset value (NAV). Brokerage commissions may apply and will reduce returns. ETFs may be subject to the following additional risks:

Market Risk: The value of an ETF may decline due to broad market fluctuations unrelated to the underlying securities.
Liquidity Risk: Some ETFs may have limited trading volume, which could make it difficult to buy or sell shares at a desired price.
Tracking Error Risk: An ETF may not perfectly replicate the performance of its benchmark index.
Concentration Risk: Sector or thematic ETFs may be concentrated in a particular industry or geography, increasing volatility.
Currency Risk: ETFs that invest in international securities may be affected by exchange rate fluctuations.
Leverage and Inverse Risk: Leveraged and inverse ETFs are designed for short-term trading and may not be suitable for long-term investors. These products use derivatives and may experience significant losses.

No Warranty
While efforts are made to ensure the accuracy of information presented, no warranties are made regarding completeness, accuracy, or timeliness. Information may change without notice.
Not a Fiduciary
This site does not act as a fiduciary on behalf of any user. Users are encouraged to consult with a registered investment advisor, financial planner, or other qualified professional before making any investment decisions.

title: Should You Buy, Or Sell Fidelity’s MSCI Industrials Index ETF (FIDU) Today?
content:
Quick Read

Fidelity MSCI Industrials Index ETF (FIDU) delivered 9.13% year-to-date returns as industrial companies benefit from AI infrastructure buildout spending, though the author recommends selling FIDU in favor of more targeted industrial exposure; Nvidia (NVDA) comprises nearly 8% of the S&P 500 with tech at 35% of the index, creating concentration risk that makes industrial stocks relatively safer during potential AI-related selloffs. Hyperscalers’ massive capital expenditures for AI buildout are flowing directly into industrial companies that manufacture construction materials, electrical components, and HVAC systems, positioning industrial stocks to outperform for the next couple of years despite broader market correlation. The analyst who called NVIDIA in 2010 just named his top 10 stocks and Fidelity MSCI Industrials Index ETF wasn't one of them. Get them here FREE.

The Fidelity MSCI Industrials Index ETF (NYSEARCA:FIDU) is a low-cost ETF that gets you exposure to some of the premier industrial and defense stocks in the market. Most investors view it as a solid play due to surging defense spending and reindustrialization. And while that hasn't paid off with the S&P 500 still ahead, I'd argue FIDU is worth taking a second look at.

The future could be bright for FIDU for multiple reasons, and some unique characteristics can turn it into a winner.

Industrial stocks are quite hardy in the current environment. Plus, you're likely underweight on them by a large margin, and you could miss out significantly if the reshoring + reindustrialization plays out as expected in the coming years.

The analyst who called NVIDIA in 2010 just named his top 10 stocks and Fidelity MSCI Industrials Index ETF wasn't one of them.Get them here FREE.

But even all that might not make it a buy in the end. Let's first take a look at what's going on.

FIDU is doing better and better

The past performance might turn off some people just because this ETF underperformed a little, but this is a mistake. In fact, I find it quite impressive that FIDU has managed to almost keep up with the S&P 500 and has actually delivered higher year-to-date returns so far this year at 9.13% vs. 5.8%.

FIDU holds stocks that went through a record-high interest rate hike cycle, plus the tariff drama. The S&P 500 went through the same, but the industrial sector never had Wall Street throwing money at it because of AI.

Things are changing, though. The premium Wall Street is paying for AI is shifting away from software tech companies into hardware and industrial businesses that are on the receiving end of the buildout money.

Story Continues

Why FIDU is set to keep outperforming

Hyperscalers are posting solid earnings results and revenue growth metrics today, but the true cost of this buildout is on the cash flow and balance sheet sections since earnings tend to spread out the expenditures.

When you look at cash flow, you'll notice many of them are already in net debt, while others are hurdling towards it. Free cash flow is continuously going down due to rising capex.

Where is all of this cash going? Right here, at least a good chunk of it. FIDU has 364 industrial holdings. This includes construction companies, electrical component manufacturers, HVAC cooling companies... This buildout touches a lot of industrials. I would not be surprised if this translates into FIDU outperforming for a couple more years.

Tech can go wrong, but industrials (probably) won't

Industrial stocks have largely kept up with the broader indexes over the past few years. You're not going to see staggering outperformance, but what you will see is more safety. Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) alone now constitutes nearly 8% of the S&P 500, with the broader tech sector's weight at 35%. AI is unlikely to run out of steam anytime soon, but when it does, you're in for a lot less trouble if you're invested in FIDU.

But again, you're still going to have market correlation due to how many holdings this ETF has. For example, FIDU declined the same as the SPY did in 2022, though it recovered a little faster. The lack of tech stocks did not mean that this ETF was spared by the selloff that ensued after the 2021 tech bubble.

Similarly, you could argue that a similar AI selloff will not spare industrials either. I'd still disagree and say you're going to see FIDU decline much less than the SPY, as industrial companies are sitting on more cash vs their tech counterparts.

Should you go ahead and buy?

No.

I did say that this ETF is doing better and better and will likely even outperform the S&P 500 this year and perhaps next year, but that's not really a reason to buy. Sure, it's doing well, but is it doing well enough for you to allocate a meaningful chunk of your portfolio to it? I don't think so.

The sheer amount of industrial holdings here leads to dilution beyond the industrial stocks you should be targeting. As I said earlier, this AI buildout is touching many industrial stocks; you should be leaning into the precise stocks that are benefiting from it instead of trying to buy the whole sector.

Thus, I'd sell FIDU and buy something like the Defiance AI & Power Infrastructure ETF (NASDAQ:AIPO). This ETF is 58% industrials, less than 20% tech, and it is up 45% year-to-date.

The analyst who called NVIDIA in 2010 just named his top 10 AI stocks

This analyst's 2025 picks are up 106% on average. He just named his top 10 stocks to buy in 2026. Get them here FREE.

title: AI Needs Power. This Soaring ETF Has the Right Stuff.
content:
These days, it sure feels as though the word "bottleneck" is joined at the hip with the artificial intelligence (AI) investment thesis. Indeed, AI adopters and enablers face a variety of logjams that threaten their ambitions.

Data centers, hardware, and power infrastructure rank high on the list of AI constraints, and an array of AI stocks offers investors avenues to capitalize on various AI hurdles. However, not all investors want to engage in stock-picking, and spreading cash across a slew of bets isn't capital-efficient for some market participants.

Will AI create the world's first trillionaire? Our team just released a report on a little-known company, called an "Indispensable Monopoly," providing the critical technology Nvidia and Intel both need. 

Continue »

Select exchange-traded funds (ETFs) solve that problem. Enter: the Defiance AI & Power Infrastructure ETF (NASDAQ: AIPO). Let's learn more about it.This ETF taps multiple corners of the AI ecosystem. Image source: Getty Images.

As its name implies, this fund is at the right place at the right time. That's reflected by the fact that this ETF is just 10 months old and already has just over $665 million in assets under management (AUM). Nearly two-thirds of that AUM tally arrived in the fund this year, confirming investors want in on the AI bottleneck story.

AIPO lives up to its billing

Simply because "AI" is attached to an ETF's name doesn't guarantee the fund is adequately levered to the theme. Fortunately, that's not a concern with the Defiance, as it delivers on its promise to address AI and power infrastructure.

The ETF offers a decent level of AI purity, allocating about 20% of its portfolio to semiconductor stocks and data infrastructure names, with Broadcom and Nvidia ranking among the top 10 holdings. The power infrastructure sleeve is where this ETF shines, as it devotes approximately 80% of its portfolio to power grid equipment, engineering and construction services, and utilities stocks.

No, this isn't your grandfather's utilities ETF, but investors should be mindful of the Defiance fund's significant power infrastructure exposure, which is one of the most crucial AI bottlenecks. Due in large part to the demands created by AI, power industry investments surged to $1.5 trillion last year, and a wide array of power sources are being affected by AI, prompting some experts to predict that the infrastructure spending cycle won't just create power, it'll mint new fortunes for smart investors, too.

Some estimates indicate AI workloads could drive data center electricity consumption to 470 terawatt-hours by 2030, potentially yielding lucrative long-term benefits for companies such as GE Vernova and Eaton, both of which are prime power infrastructure names and top 10 holdings in the Defiance ETF.

Story Continues

Efficiently capitalize on AI bottlenecks

No pun intended, but one of the primary advantages of this ETF is utility. The fund, which is up nearly 46% year to date, holds 77 stocks. That's a fairly deep bench among thematic ETFs and is reflective of the fund's efforts to address multiple themes under one umbrella.

No, this ETF isn't "perfect." Still, it's efficient and relevant, particularly at a time when AI is forecast to prompt the addition of hundreds of terawatt-hours (TWh) of energy consumption over the next several years. That means multiple power sources must be embraced, and grid infrastructure must be enhanced. This ETF leans into those themes and others.

The Defiance AI & Power Infrastructure ETF charges 0.69% per year, or $69 on a $10,000 investment.

Should you buy stock in Tidal Trust II - Defiance Ai & Power Infrastructure ETF right now?

Before you buy stock in Tidal Trust II - Defiance Ai & Power Infrastructure ETF, consider this:

The Motley Fool Stock Advisor analyst team just identified what they believe are the 10 best stocks for investors to buy now… and Tidal Trust II - Defiance Ai & Power Infrastructure ETF wasn’t one of them. The 10 stocks that made the cut could produce monster returns in the coming years.

Consider when Netflix made this list on December 17, 2004... if you invested $1,000 at the time of our recommendation, you’d have $483,476!* Or when Nvidia made this list on April 15, 2005... if you invested $1,000 at the time of our recommendation, you’d have $1,362,941!*

Now, it’s worth noting Stock Advisor’s total average return is 998% — a market-crushing outperformance compared to 207% for the S&P 500. Don't miss the latest top 10 list, available with Stock Advisor, and join an investing community built by individual investors for individual investors.

See the 10 stocks »

*Stock Advisor returns as of May 20, 2026.

Todd Shriber has no position in any of the stocks mentioned. The Motley Fool has positions in and recommends Broadcom, Eaton Plc, GE Vernova, and Nvidia. The Motley Fool has a disclosure policy.
